**Shubhdeep Singh Sidhu** (11 June 1993 – 29 May 2022), known professionally as **Sidhu Moose Wala**,[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Scroll_4Jun2022-6) was an Indian singer and rapper. He worked predominantly in [Punjabi-language music](/wiki/Punjabi_Music "Punjabi Music") and [cinema](/wiki/Punjabi_cinema "Punjabi cinema"). Moose Wala is considered to be one of the most influential and successful [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabis "Punjabis") rappers of all time[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) and to many, among the greatest Indian musicians of his generation.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 2020, Moose Wala was named by *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* among 50 up and coming artists.[[13]](#cite_note-13) He also became the first Punjabi and Indian singer to perform at [Wireless Festival](/wiki/Wireless_Festival "Wireless Festival") and won four awards at the [Brit Asia TV Music Awards](/wiki/Brit_Asia_TV_Music_Awards "Brit Asia TV Music Awards").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Moose Wala rose to mainstream popularity with his track "So High". In 2018, he released his debut album *[PBX 1](/wiki/PBX_1 "PBX 1")*, which peaked at number 66 on the [*Billboard* Canadian Albums](/wiki/Canadian_Albums_Chart "Canadian Albums Chart") chart. His singles "[47](/wiki/47_(song) "47 (song)")" and "[Mera Na](/wiki/Mera_Na "Mera Na")" were ranked on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart").

Born in [Moosa, Punjab](/wiki/Moosa,_Punjab "Moosa, Punjab"), Moose Wala began his career in 2016 as a songwriter for the song "License" by [Ninja](/wiki/Ninja_(singer) "Ninja (singer)"), and as lead artist in 2017 with Gurlez Akhtar for a duet song, "[G Wagon](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_G-Class "Mercedes-Benz G-Class")". Following his debut, he collaborated with Brown Boyz for various tracks. Moose Wala's tracks peaked on the [UK Asian](/wiki/Official_Charts_Company "Official Charts Company") Music chart. His song "Bambiha Bole" was among the top five on the Global [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") music chart. In 2021, he released *[Moosetape](/wiki/Moosetape "Moosetape")*, tracks from which charted globally including on the [*Billboard* Global 200](/wiki/Billboard_Global_200 "Billboard Global 200"), [*Billboard* Global Excl. US](/wiki/Billboard_Global_Excl._US "Billboard Global Excl. US"), [Canadian Hot 100](/wiki/Canadian_Hot_100 "Canadian Hot 100"), UK Asian, and New Zealand Hot charts. He has the most number-one singles on the [*Billboard* India Songs](/wiki/Billboard_India_Songs "Billboard India Songs") chart. It became the first Indian album to have more than 1 billion streams on [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2021, Moose Wala joined the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") (INC) political party and unsuccessfully contested the [2022 Punjab Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2022_Punjab_Legislative_Assembly_election "2022 Punjab Legislative Assembly election") for [Mansa](/wiki/Mansa,_Punjab_Assembly_constituency "Mansa, Punjab Assembly constituency").

He was shot dead by unidentified assailants on 29 May 2022; a Canada-based gangster, known as [Goldy Brar](/wiki/Goldy_Brar "Goldy Brar") and a member of the [Lawrence Bishnoi](/wiki/Lawrence_Bishnoi "Lawrence Bishnoi") gang, claimed responsibility for the killing, which the police said was the culmination of an inter-gang rivalry.[[16]](#cite_note-16) On 23 June 2022, his first posthumous single, "[SYL](/wiki/SYL_(song) "SYL (song)")", was released.

Moose Wala's lyrics and themes promoting gun culture and violence were often seen as controversial in India.[[17]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-17) Subsequently, he had faced legal challenges for his support of gun culture and his inflammatory lyrics.[[18]](#cite_note-indianexpress.com-18)

## Early life

Shubhdeep Singh Sidhu was born in the [village of Moosa](/wiki/Moosa,_Mansa_district "Moosa, Mansa district") in the [Mansa district](/wiki/Mansa_district,_India "Mansa district, India") of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), India to Balkaur Singh and his mother is Charan Kaur, members of the [Jat Sikh](/wiki/Jat_Sikh "Jat Sikh") community.[[6]](#cite_note-Scroll_4Jun2022-6)[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-ITNov2019-21)[[22]](#cite_note-NDTV_31May2022-22)

Moose Wala studied at [Guru Nanak Dev Engineering College, Ludhiana](/wiki/Guru_Nanak_Dev_Engineering_College,_Ludhiana "Guru Nanak Dev Engineering College, Ludhiana") and graduated with a degree in [electrical engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering") in 2016.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)[[23]](#cite_note-23) He admired and was influenced by rapper [Tupac Shakur](/wiki/Tupac_Shakur "Tupac Shakur").[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)[[21]](#cite_note-ITNov2019-21) He started listening to [hip-hop music](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") as a student in sixth grade, and was trained in music by Harvinder Bittu in [Ludhiana](/wiki/Ludhiana "Ludhiana").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) According to statements he made while campaigning, he chose *Sidhu Moose Wala* ([Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"): ਸਿੱਧੂ ਮੂਸੇ ਵਾਲਾ, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Sidhu from Moosa') for his stage name as a tribute to his home village of Moosa.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

After graduation, Moose Wala moved to [Brampton, Ontario](/wiki/Brampton,_Ontario "Brampton, Ontario"), Canada, as an [international student](/wiki/International_students_in_Canada "International students in Canada").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) While living there he studied at [Humber College](/wiki/Humber_College "Humber College").[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[Note 1]](#cite_note-33)

## Career

### 2017–2018: Rise to fame

Moose Wala released his first song "G Wagon" in 2017 while living in Brampton.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) Later that year, he had his breakthrough with the song "So High", a [gangster rap](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap") with music producer [Byg Byrd](/wiki/Byg_Byrd "Byg Byrd").[[33]](#cite_note-:3-34) The song won him the 2017 Best Lyricist award at the [Brit Asia TV Music Awards](/wiki/Brit_Asia_TV_Music_Awards "Brit Asia TV Music Awards").[[34]](#cite_note-35) Following this, he joined Brown Boys Records along with [Sunny Malton](/wiki/Sunny_Malton "Sunny Malton") and Byg Byrd.

He began to perform live shows in India in 2018, and performed numerous shows in Canada.[[35]](#cite_note-36) He continued his success with singles like "Issa Jatt", "It's All About You", and "Just Listen".[[33]](#cite_note-:3-34) In May 2018, he released the single "Tochan", followed by "Famous" which entered the [UK Asian Top 40 Chart](/wiki/Official_Charts_Company "Official Charts Company").[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

At the 2018 [PTC Punjabi Music Awards](/wiki/PTC_Punjabi_Music_Awards "PTC Punjabi Music Awards"), he was nominated for the Best New Age Sensation award for "Issa Jatt".[[38]](#cite_note-39) In August 2018, he released his first film soundtrack song, "Dollar", for the film *[Dakuaan Da Munda](/wiki/Dakuaan_Da_Munda "Dakuaan Da Munda")*.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Following various successful songs with [music publisher](/wiki/Music_publisher "Music publisher") Humble Music, he began releasing songs independently in 2018, starting with "Warning Shots", a [diss track](/wiki/Diss_track "Diss track") targeting [Karan Aujla](/wiki/Karan_Aujla "Karan Aujla")'s track "Lafaafe".[[40]](#cite_note-:4-41)

In October 2018, he released his debut album *[PBX 1](/wiki/PBX_1 "PBX 1")* under [T-Series](/wiki/T-Series_(company) "T-Series (company)"), in the [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") genre with [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") influences. The album charted on the [*Billboard* Canadian Albums](/wiki/Canadian_Albums_Chart "Canadian Albums Chart") chart,[[41]](#cite_note-42) and won the Best Album Award at the 2019 Brit Asia TV Music Awards.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The album was followed by the release of most of his tracks under his own label, as well as tracks from other artists.

### 2019–2022: Established singer

In February 2019 Moose Wala released "Legend" under his own record label, and it won the [Brit Asia TV](/wiki/Brit_Asia_TV "Brit Asia TV") Kuflink Best Track of the Year Award that year.[[43]](#cite_note-44)In the same month, he released "Chosen" and "Outlaw", and in April, he released "East Side Flow", which was followed by "Mafia Style" with Aman Hayer. In June, his concert at the Surrey Music Festival was cancelled due to security concerns stemming from violence at his previous performances.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

In August, he collaborated with [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia_(rapper) "Bohemia (rapper)") on "Same Beef", which was a huge hit.[[45]](#cite_note-46) In September, he released two film soundtrack songs: "Dogar" for *Teri Meri Jodi*[[46]](#cite_note-47) and "Jatti Jeone Morh Wargi", featuring [Sonam Bajwa](/wiki/Sonam_Bajwa "Sonam Bajwa"), for *[Ardab Mutiyaran](/wiki/Ardab_Mutiyaran "Ardab Mutiyaran")*.[[47]](#cite_note-48) In October, he released "[47](/wiki/47_(song) "47 (song)")", featuring British rappers [Mist](/wiki/Mist_(rapper) "Mist (rapper)") and [Stefflon Don](/wiki/Stefflon_Don "Stefflon Don"), which entered the top 20 on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart").[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) The song also charted on the [New Zealand top 40 singles](/wiki/Official_New_Zealand_Music_Chart "Official New Zealand Music Chart") chart.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

In 2019, [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") included him in its list of the most popular artists in Punjab, along with [Maninder Buttar](/wiki/Maninder_Buttar "Maninder Buttar") and Karan Aujla.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

In January 2020, Moose Wala was featured along with [Nseeb](/wiki/Nseeb "Nseeb") on [Prem Dhillon](/wiki/Prem_Dhillon "Prem Dhillon")'s track "Old Skool".[[52]](#cite_note-Old_Skool_by_Sidhu_Moosewala_on_YouTube-53) The song was followed by "Tibeyan Da Putt", which topped the [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes") charts and was ranked at number 8 on the [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music") charts in India.[[53]](#cite_note-54) His second studio album, *[Snitches Get Stitches](/wiki/Snitches_Get_Stitches "Snitches Get Stitches")*, was released under his own label in May 2020.[[54]](#cite_note-55) That same month, he released the single "Dear Mama" on his mother's birthday.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In June 2020, he collaborated with [Amrit Maan](/wiki/Amrit_Maan "Amrit Maan") on the song "Bambiha Bole".[[56]](#cite_note-57) whose music video was viewed over ten million times within twenty-four hours.[[57]](#cite_note-58) The song reached number one in India and entered the top 50 in Canada and New Zealand on the Apple Music charts.[[58]](#cite_note-:6-59)[[59]](#cite_note-:7-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)It topped the UK Asian chart and also entered the top 5 of the Global YouTube charts.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) On 31 August 2020, he officially launched his record label, 5911 Records.[[63]](#cite_note-64)In September 2020, he released "Game" with [Shooter Kahlon](/wiki/Shooter_Kahlon "Shooter Kahlon"). It was his first song to appear on the [Canadian Hot 100](/wiki/Canadian_Hot_100 "Canadian Hot 100") chart by [Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)"). Also, "Game" is the most commented Indian song on YouTube with over 5 million comments.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

In May 2021, Moose Wala released his third studio album, *[Moosetape](/wiki/Moosetape "Moosetape")*. The album charted on the New Zealand Top 40 Albums chart by *[Recorded Music NZ](/wiki/Recorded_Music_NZ "Recorded Music NZ")*.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Singles from the album charted on various international charts including the [*Billboard* Global 200](/wiki/Billboard_Global_200 "Billboard Global 200"), Canadian Hot 100, and New Zealand Hot Singles charts. On 12 September 2021, he performed at the [Wireless Festival](/wiki/Wireless_Festival "Wireless Festival") in London with Mist. Moose Wala was the first Indian singer to perform at this festival.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

In April 2022, Moose Wala released the [EP](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play") *[No Name](/wiki/No_Name_(EP) "No Name (EP)")* featuring [AR Paisley](/wiki/AR_Paisley "AR Paisley"), [Mr. Capone-E](/wiki/Mr._Capone-E "Mr. Capone-E") and Sunny Malton,[[67]](#cite_note-68) The EP performed well on various music charts. It reached number 50 on the Canadian Albums Chart, and the song "Never Fold" debuted at number 92 on the Canadian Hot 100 chart. The songs also charted internationally. In New Zealand, "Never Fold" peaked at number 19, while "0 to 100" and "Love Sick" reached numbers 34 and 39, respectively. In the UK, "Never Fold" topped the Punjabi chart at number 4, followed by "0-100" at number 9 and "Everybody Hurts" at number 10.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

Before his death, Sidhu Moose Wala released two songs, "[The Last Ride](/wiki/The_Last_Ride_(song) "The Last Ride (song)")" and "[Levels](/wiki/Levels_(Sidhu_Moose_Wala_song) "Levels (Sidhu Moose Wala song)")". "Levels" was his last song, released in May 2022. These songs made it to the Billboard Canadian Hot 100 chart. "The Last Ride" reached number 26 and "Levels" reached number 32. Additionally, both songs appeared on the Billboard Global Excl. US chart, with "The Last Ride" at number 103 and "Levels" at number 195.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

### Posthumous Releases

In June 2022, his single "[SYL](/wiki/SYL_(song) "SYL (song)")" (a reference to the [Satluj Yamuna link canal](/wiki/Sutlej_Yamuna_link_canal "Sutlej Yamuna link canal")) posthumously peaked at 27 on the Canadian Hot 100, 81 in Australia, 3 in India, and 200 on the Global Excl. US chart.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

In April 2023, Sidhu's unreleased song "[Mera Na](/wiki/Mera_Na "Mera Na")", a collaboration with [Burna Boy](/wiki/Burna_Boy "Burna Boy"), was released posthumously. The song charted on several international music charts. In Canada, it peaked at number 14 on the Canadian Hot 100 chart on 22 April 2023. Globally, the song reached number 102 on the Billboard Global Excl. US chart. In New Zealand, it peaked at number 2 on the NZ Hot Singles Chart. In the UK, the song reached number 87 on the UK Singles Chart and number 27 on the UK Indie Singles Chart.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

"Chorni", a collaboration with [Divine](/wiki/Divine_(rapper) "Divine (rapper)"), was released in July 2023 and went on to chart on several international music charts. The song achieved notable success, peaking at number 27 on the Canada Hot 100 chart and number 7 on the NZ Hot Singles Chart.[[73]](#cite_note-74) Another song, "Watch Out", a collaboration with Sikander Kahlon, was released in November 2023, which also gained significant traction, reaching number 33 on the Canada Hot 100 chart, number 11 on the New Zealand Hot Singles Chart, and number 3 on the UK Asian Chart.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

Sidhu's song "Drippy", a collaboration with [AR Paisley](/wiki/AR_Paisley "AR Paisley"), was released in February 2024. The song charted on several international music charts. In Canada, it peaked at number 9 on the Canadian Hot 100 chart. Globally, the song reached number 152 on the Billboard Global Excluding US chart. In New Zealand, it peaked at number 11 on the NZ Hot Singles Chart and number 1 on the UK Asian Singles Chart.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Sidhu released another titled "410" in collaboration with [Sunny Malton](/wiki/Sunny_Malton "Sunny Malton") in April 2024. He also collaborated with [Fredo](/wiki/Fredo_(rapper) "Fredo (rapper)") and [Steel Banglez](/wiki/Steel_Banglez "Steel Banglez") on a song released in August 2023. Both songs charted on the Canada Hot 100, New Zealand, and UK music charts.

### Acting

Moose Wala made his debut in [Punjabi cinema](/wiki/Punjabi_cinema "Punjabi cinema") with the film *[Yes I Am Student](/wiki/Yes_I_Am_Student "Yes I Am Student")* under his own production company Jatt Life Studios.[[77]](#cite_note-78) The film was directed by [Tarnvir Singh Jagpal](/wiki/Tarnvir_Singh_Jagpal "Tarnvir Singh Jagpal") and written by Gill Raunta.[[78]](#cite_note-79) In 2019, Moose Wala appeared in *Teri Meri Jodi*.[[79]](#cite_note-80) In June 2020, he announced another film titled *Gunah*.[[80]](#cite_note-81) On 22 August 2021, he released the teaser of his upcoming movie, *[Moosa Jatt](/wiki/Moosa_Jatt "Moosa Jatt")*, starring Sweetaj Brar and directed by Tru Makers.[[81]](#cite_note-82) On 24 August 2021, he announced his new film *Jattan Da Munda Gaun Lagya*, directed by [Amberdeep Singh](/wiki/Amberdeep_Singh "Amberdeep Singh"), which was set for release on 18 March 2022.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

## Public Image

Sidhu Moose Wala's public image was complex. As a celebrated music icon, he was admired for his unique style and authenticity. However, his lyrics often sparked controversy, with critics accusing him of promoting violence. Despite this, Sidhu Moose Wala remained steadfast and defended his views, subsequently earning a strong following and leaving a lasting impact on Punjabi music.[[83]](#cite_note-84) [Raja Kumari](/wiki/Raja_Kumari "Raja Kumari") called Sidhu Moose Wala her "Gentle Giant", stating, "He played a huge role in popularizing Punjabi music and creating opportunities for female artists like me."[[84]](#cite_note-85) [Honey Singh](/wiki/Honey_Singh "Honey Singh") credited Sidhu Moose Wala for taking [Punjabi Music](/wiki/Punjabi_Music "Punjabi Music") to the global stage.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Badshah](/wiki/Badshah_(rapper) "Badshah (rapper)") called Sidhu Moose Wala a legend, noting his legacy and decision to remain connected to his community roots despite achieving global success.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

## Personal life

### Family

Sidhu Moose Wala lived in his home village [Moosa](/wiki/Moosa,_Mansa_district "Moosa, Mansa district"), which his fans often visited to meet him. He was very attached to his grandmother and kept his hair long at her request, a practice considered very important in Sikhism. In contrast, his father was unable to keep his hair due to an accident.[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88) On 17 March 2024, two years after his death, Sidhu's parents had a second son via [in vitro fertilisation](/wiki/In_vitro_fertilisation "In vitro fertilisation").[[88]](#cite_note-89)

### Feuds

Moose Wala had a rivalry with Karan Aujla; both have replied to each other through songs, on social media, and in live performances.[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91) Both have also been criticised for songs promoting violence.[[91]](#cite_note-92) In an interview their mutual colleague Elly Mangat disclosed that the dispute between them began when Moose Wala's video targeting Aujla in his song was leaked to Aujla's management, and they threatened to attack Moose Wala. Following the incident, both started targeting each other on social media.[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) The rivalry was resolved temporarily until Aujla released a diss track, "Lafaafe", which Moose Wala responded to with "Warning Shots".[[40]](#cite_note-:4-41) Aujla in an interview stated that he did not write the track "Lafaafe" and did not reveal anything about their rivalry, but praised Moose Wala's work.[[94]](#cite_note-95) After his death, Aujla paid tribute with the song "Maa".[[95]](#cite_note-96)

### Legal issues and controversies

At the time of his death, Moose Wala was facing criminal charges for promoting gun culture and violence. Two of the charges were related to obscene scenes.[[96]](#cite_note-Tribune_News_dead-97)

In May 2020, two videos featuring him went viral on social media: one showed him training to use an [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47") with assistance from police officers,[[97]](#cite_note-98) and the other showed him using a personal pistol.[[98]](#cite_note-99) The six officers who had assisted him were suspended following the incident.[[99]](#cite_note-100) On 19 May, he was booked under two sections of the [Arms Act](/wiki/Arms_Act,_1959 "Arms Act, 1959").[[100]](#cite_note-101) The police began conducting raids to find Moose Wala, but he hid to evade arrest.[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104) On 2 June, the [Barnala](/wiki/Barnala_district "Barnala district") District Court rejected a plea for [anticipatory bail](/wiki/Anticipatory_bail "Anticipatory bail") for Moose Wala and five accused officers.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

On 6 June 2020, Moose Wala was fined by police in [Nabha](/wiki/Nabha "Nabha") because his car windows were tinted darker than what was permissible, and he was allowed to leave despite being wanted on outstanding charges; he incorrectly told the officers that he was already out on bail.[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5) In July, he joined the police investigation and was granted regular bail.[[105]](#cite_note-106) That month, he released a single titled "Sanju", comparing himself to actor [Sanjay Dutt](/wiki/Sanjay_Dutt "Sanjay Dutt"), who was also arrested under the Arms Act.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Indian sport shooter [Avneet Sidhu](/wiki/Avneet_Sidhu "Avneet Sidhu") criticised the song and called out Moose Wala for promoting gun culture.[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109) The next day, a case was registered against him for releasing the song.[[109]](#cite_note-110) In an interview, Moose Wala alleged that he was being deliberately targeted by some news channels and lawyers.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25)

### References to Mai Bhago

In September 2019, his song "Jatti Jeone Morh Wargi" was deemed inappropriate by [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") leaders for using the name of [Mai Bhago](/wiki/Mai_Bhago "Mai Bhago"), a 17th-century Sikh warrior woman.[[110]](#cite_note-IE_31May2022-111)[[111]](#cite_note-Tribune_Sept2019-112) Sikh delegations and [Akali Dal](/wiki/Akali_Dal "Akali Dal") leaders demanded a ban on the song, subsequently lodging complaints against Moose Wala in Mansa and [Bathinda](/wiki/Bathinda "Bathinda").[[111]](#cite_note-Tribune_Sept2019-112) Moose Wala later apologised on social media and in March 2020 appeared before the Sikh religious body [Akal Takht](/wiki/Akal_Takht "Akal Takht") in a hearing over the incident.[[110]](#cite_note-IE_31May2022-111)

### References to Khalistan movement

In December 2020, Moose Wala released the single "Panjab: My Motherland" in support of the [Indian farmers protest](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932021_Indian_farmers%27_protest "2020–2021 Indian farmers' protest") against the [2020 Indian agriculture acts](/wiki/2020_Indian_agriculture_acts "2020 Indian agriculture acts"), which featured clips of orthodox Sikh militant [Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale](/wiki/Jarnail_Singh_Bhindranwale "Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale") and speeches made by [Khalistan](/wiki/Khalistan_movement "Khalistan movement") supporter Bharpur Singh Balbir in the late 1980s.[[112]](#cite_note-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115) In an interview Moose Wala said that Khalistan means a 'pure place' ([Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language"): पवित्र-स्थान), like it was under the rule of [Maharaja Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh "Maharaja Ranjit Singh"), where people of all religions lived in harmony. Similarly Sidhu clarified in the interview that Khalistan, to him, means the rule of Maharaja Ranjit Singh, not a separate country. He emphasised that he is an Indian citizen, living in India, and has a deep connection to the country.[[115]](#cite_note-116)[[116]](#cite_note-117)

## Politics

Moose Wala actively campaigned for his mother, Charan Kaur, who won the [sarpanch](/wiki/Sarpanch "Sarpanch") election for Moosa in December 2018.[[117]](#cite_note-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Sidhu_Moose_Wala_Congress.png/250px-Sidhu_Moose_Wala_Congress.png)](/wiki/File:Sidhu_Moose_Wala_Congress.png)

Moose Wala joining [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") in the presence of Chief Minister [Charanjit Singh Channi](/wiki/Charanjit_Singh_Channi "Charanjit Singh Channi") and PPCC President [Navjot Singh Sidhu](/wiki/Navjot_Singh_Sidhu "Navjot Singh Sidhu").

On 3 December 2021, Moose Wala joined the [INC](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") to contest the [2022 Punjab Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2022_Punjab_Legislative_Assembly_election "2022 Punjab Legislative Assembly election").[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120) Nazar Singh Manshahia, the INC assemblyman from Mansa, revolted and opposed Moose Wala's candidacy.[[120]](#cite_note-cremated-121) Obtaining only 20.52% votes from the [Mansa constituency](/wiki/Mansa,_Punjab_Assembly_constituency "Mansa, Punjab Assembly constituency"), Moose Wala lost to the [Aam Aadmi Party](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi_Party "Aam Aadmi Party")'s [Vijay Singla](/wiki/Vijay_Singla "Vijay Singla") by a margin of 63,323 votes.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

During the 2022 election, a case under Section 188 of the [Indian Penal Code](/wiki/Indian_Penal_Code "Indian Penal Code") was filed against Moose Wala for his violation of the election code of conduct. He had held a door-to-door campaign in Mansa after the campaigning deadline.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

On 11 April 2022, Moose Wala released a song titled "Scapegoat", in which he laments his failure in the 2022 elections.[[123]](#cite_note-124) The Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) claimed that Moose Wala insinuated through his song that the voters of Punjab were *gadara* (Punjabi: ਗੱਦਾਰ, lit. 'traitors') for electing the AAP. They also claimed that his song perpetuated the INC's "anti-Punjab" mentality and demanded an answer from the party's state unit president, [Amrinder Singh Raja Warring](/wiki/Amrinder_Singh_Raja_Warring "Amrinder Singh Raja Warring"), on whether he endorsed Moose Wala's views.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

## Assassination

Main article: [Assassination of Sidhu Moose Wala](/wiki/Assassination_of_Sidhu_Moose_Wala "Assassination of Sidhu Moose Wala")

| Assassination of Sidhu Moose Wala | |
| --- | --- |
| Location | Jawaharke Village, [Mansa](/wiki/Mansa,_Punjab "Mansa, Punjab"), [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), India |
| Date | 29 May 2022; 3 years ago (2022-05-29)  5:30 p.m. ([IST](/wiki/Indian_Standard_Time "Indian Standard Time")) |
| Target | Sidhu Moose Wala |
| Attack type | [Murder](/wiki/Murder "Murder") by [drive-by shooting](/wiki/Drive-by_shooting "Drive-by shooting"), [assassination](/wiki/Assassination "Assassination") |
| Deaths | 1 (Sidhu Moose Wala) |
| Injured | 2 |

Moose Wala was shot dead by unidentified assailants in his car on 29 May 2022 in Jawaharke village of Mansa at age of 28. According to police, [Lawrence Bishnoi](/wiki/Lawrence_Bishnoi "Lawrence Bishnoi")'s gang initially claimed responsibility for the murder in an unverified [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") post, which Bishnoi denied making,[[128]](#cite_note-129) and he was being held by the [Punjab Police](/wiki/Punjab_Police_(India) "Punjab Police (India)") as of June 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sidhu_Moose_Wala&action=edit) and was considered the "mastermind" of the murder by officials.[[129]](#cite_note-130)[[130]](#cite_note-131)

According to police, at around 5:10 pm, Moose Wala left his house with his cousin Gurpreet Singh and neighbour Gurwinder Singh. Moose Wala was driving his black [Mahindra Thar](/wiki/Mahindra_Thar "Mahindra Thar") SUV to his aunt's house in [Barnala](/wiki/Barnala "Barnala").[[131]](#cite_note-NDTV_details-132) At 5:30 pm when the SUV reached Jawaharke, two other cars intercepted and blocked it.[[132]](#cite_note-NDTV_30_May_Shot-133) Thirty rounds were fired during the incident, which also injured two other men.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Moose Wala fired back at the attackers using his pistol. After the shootout, the attackers left the scene.[[131]](#cite_note-NDTV_details-132) His father took Moose Wala to the civil hospital in [Mansa](/wiki/Mansa,_Punjab "Mansa, Punjab"), where he was declared dead.[[134]](#cite_note-indianexpress_death-135)

Moose Wala was among the 424 people whose police security was reduced or entirely removed the day before, in preparation for the anniversary of [Operation Blue Star](/wiki/Operation_Blue_Star "Operation Blue Star"), leaving him with two commandos instead of the earlier four.[[134]](#cite_note-indianexpress_death-135)[[135]](#cite_note-136) At the time of the incident, Moose Wala was travelling in his private car accompanied by two others instead of his bullet-proof vehicle with the commandos.[[136]](#cite_note-10_Facts-137)[[137]](#cite_note-TNS-138) According to his friends, Moose Wala did not take his security along with him, as his Thar SUV could not accommodate five people.[[131]](#cite_note-NDTV_details-132)

### Aftermath

According to police, the Bishnoi gang claimed they killed Moose Wala to avenge the murder of an Akali leader, Vicky Middukhera, in 2021. Moose Wala's aide was purported by the Bishnoi gang of having a role in the killing of Middukhera, though there is no legal evidence to support this.[[132]](#cite_note-NDTV_30_May_Shot-133) Canadian [gangster](/wiki/Gangster "Gangster") of Punjabi origin Satinder Singh a.k.a. [Goldy Brar](/wiki/Goldy_Brar "Goldy Brar") claimed responsibility for the murder. Brar, a close associate of Bishnoi, claimed that his "[Punjab module](/wiki/Organized_crime_in_India#Organized_crime_in_Punjab "Organized crime in India")" carried out the shooting. Both Brar and Bishnoi have criminal cases against them in India. The Punjab Police later confirmed Bishnoi's involvement.[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141)

According to the police report, Moose Wala's father revealed that Moose Wala had been receiving death threats from gangsters for extortion purposes, a statement corroborated by singer [Mika Singh](/wiki/Mika_Singh "Mika Singh").[[141]](#cite_note-142)

Punjab Chief Minister [Bhagwant Mann](/wiki/Bhagwant_Mann "Bhagwant Mann") condemned the murder. Calling Moose Wala "a cultural icon of Punjab", he expressed shock and grief about his death and condoled the aggrieved family.[[142]](#cite_note-judicial_commission-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144) Mann ordered an investigation into why Moose Wala's security was reduced by the Punjab Police two days prior.[[132]](#cite_note-NDTV_30_May_Shot-133) He also announced the setting up of a judicial commission headed by a sitting judge of the [Punjab and Haryana High Court](/wiki/Punjab_and_Haryana_High_Court "Punjab and Haryana High Court") to investigate the killing.[[142]](#cite_note-judicial_commission-143) Numerous celebrities offered their condolences on social media.[[144]](#cite_note-145)[[145]](#cite_note-146)

Police found bullets from an [AN-94](/wiki/AN-94 "AN-94") Russian assault rifle and a pistol at the spot of the killing. Police had detained six suspects of the incident from the state of [Uttarakhand](/wiki/Uttarakhand "Uttarakhand").[[132]](#cite_note-NDTV_30_May_Shot-133)[[146]](#cite_note-147) On 30 May, one of the murder suspects was detained by the Punjab Police while he was hiding among the pilgrims of [Gurudwara Shri Hemkund Sahib](/wiki/Gurudwara_Shri_Hemkund_Sahib "Gurudwara Shri Hemkund Sahib").[[147]](#cite_note-148)

On 30 May, the [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") unit of the INC staged a protest near AAP leader [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal")'s residence holding the [AAP](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi_Party,_Punjab "Aam Aadmi Party, Punjab")-ruled [Government of Punjab](/wiki/Government_of_Punjab,_India "Government of Punjab, India") responsible for the incident, blaming their decision to curtail Moose Wala's security cover.[[148]](#cite_note-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150)

His autopsy was carried out by five doctors, and the event was videographed. According to post mortem reports, Moose Wala received 19 bullet injuries, and he died within 15 minutes of being shot due to the wounds.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

Moose Wala was cremated in his ancestral village on 31 May. His last rites were performed on his farmland.[[22]](#cite_note-NDTV_31May2022-22)[[151]](#cite_note-152)

On 3 June, Bishnoi allegedly admitted to being involved in the murder and that he had a rivalry with Moose Wala.[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154) On 8 June, a *[bhog](/wiki/Bhog "Bhog")* ceremony was arranged in Mansa.[[154]](#cite_note-155) On 3 July, Ankit Sirsa was arrested by Delhi police for being one of the shooters involved in the killing.[[155]](#cite_note-156) On 20 July, gangsters Manpreet Mannu and Jagrup Rupa, suspected of involvement in Moose Wala's killing, were gunned down by the Punjab Police.[[156]](#cite_note-157) On 26 July, [India TV](/wiki/India_TV "India TV") reported that Punjab Police arrested the last absconding shooter, Deepak Mundi.[[157]](#cite_note-158) However, *[The Indian Express](/wiki/The_Indian_Express "The Indian Express")* reported that he was arrested on 11 September near the Bengal-Nepal border.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

### Politics

Before the [Sangrur Lok Sabha by-election in 2022](/wiki/Sangrur_Lok_Sabha_constituency#2022_candidates "Sangrur Lok Sabha constituency"), the [INC](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") had used pictures of Moose Wala in its election song. The family of Moose Wala made a public appeal to political parties and individuals, asking them to not use his name for political or personal motives. The move was also criticised by the [Aam Aadmi Party](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi_Party "Aam Aadmi Party").[[159]](#cite_note-motives-160)

## Legacy and remembrance

* On 3 June 2022, Punjab Chief Minister Bhagwant Mann announced the construction of a cancer hospital and sports stadium in remembrance of Moose Wala.[[160]](#cite_note-161)
* On 8 June 2022, the [Brampton](/wiki/Brampton "Brampton"), Canada city council passed a motion to commission a giant mural which would be painted by a local artist and to plant a tree in the singer's honour.[[161]](#cite_note-162) In June 2023, the mural was finished.[[162]](#cite_note-163)
* In June 2022, a turban-tying competition was organised by the Shri Guru Ram Dass welfare society in [Amritsar](/wiki/Amritsar "Amritsar") in his memory, due to him being a turban-wearing Sikh.[[163]](#cite_note-164)
* [Punjabi language](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") singer Prem Dhillon released a tribute song, "Ain't died in vain", dedicated to Moose Wala on 16 June 2022.[[164]](#cite_note-165) Posthumously, on his 29th birthday, Moose Wala received a tribute from his fans when the billboards of New York City's [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") were used to play his songs.[[165]](#cite_note-166)
* [Garry Sandhu](/wiki/Garry_Sandhu "Garry Sandhu") released the tribute song "Jigar Da Tota", dedicated to Moose Wala.[[166]](#cite_note-167)
* On 5 June 2022, two murals in [California, USA](/wiki/California,_USA "California, USA") were made in tribute of Moose Wala. Rapper Bohemia also visited them and broke down in tears after an emotional tribute.[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169)
* On 17 June 2022, Canadian rapper [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)") played two of Moose Wala's singles "295" & "G-Shit" from *Moosetape* in remembrance on his debut radio show *Table for One* on [Sound42](/wiki/Sirius_XM "Sirius XM").[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) Drake later launched a t-shirt collection to honour Moose Wala, wearing one at a concert in Canada on 28 July.[[171]](#cite_note-172)
* On 25 July 2022, Punjabi Virsa, a [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")-based literary society, honoured Moose Wala with the [Waris Shah](/wiki/Waris_Shah "Waris Shah") International Award.[[172]](#cite_note-173)
* In November 2022, Nigerian artist [Burna Boy](/wiki/Burna_Boy "Burna Boy") met with Moose Wala's parents for their blessings and offered his condolences. Burna and music producer [Steel Banglez](/wiki/Steel_Banglez "Steel Banglez") honoured them with a portrait of their son made from crystals.[[173]](#cite_note-174) Burna also paid tribute to Moose Wala previously, when he broke down on stage while giving a tribute to the late singer.[[174]](#cite_note-175)[[175]](#cite_note-176)
* Sunny Malton, who was formerly in the Punjabi group Brown Boys with Moose Wala, released a tribute song "Letter to Sidhu" in November 2022.[[176]](#cite_note-177)
* In April 2023, the third posthumous release from Moose Wala, titled "Mera Na", was unveiled in advance of the one-year anniversary of his passing. The song, which features Burna Boy and Steel Banglez, was composed by the latter and quickly gained millions of plays.[[177]](#cite_note-178)
* In May 2023, award-winning British rapper [Tion Wayne](/wiki/Tion_Wayne "Tion Wayne") released the song "Healing" which paid tribute to the late Moose Wala. It features Moose Wala's father (Balkaur Singh Sidhu), his 5911 Tractor and his village of Moosa. Wayne had previously collaborated with Moose Wala on the song "Celebrity Killer" in 2022.[[178]](#cite_note-179)
